author: @erickdupont4503
text: Drawing dry air into an evap medium dampened with water would cause said air to cool significantly. Inserting a heat exchanger ( think aluminum tubing air duct) within that cold, humid air section will give you A.C. Make it double acting and you have a continuous flow of dry, cool air to supply perhaps a small room.

author: @jeffreyrood8755
text: Another idea I thought about was running the line that comes off of the valve chest through an ice chest with ice and then it would condition the air as someone had mentioned this being an air handler instead of an air conditioner. Just more thoughts.

replies.author: @easy_s3351
replies.text:
That's not a bad idea although making ice in summer costs a lot of energy. But you could of course collect ice during winter, store it in a well insulated tank (buried in the ground for instance) and then use that in the summer to cool your house. I think that's kind of what people used to do back in the day, they'd collect ice during winter and then store it and use it to keep things cool during summer. The problem is that you'd need to collect (and store) a lot of ice to cool your house all summer long.
In Germany they now have systems that use this ice collection principle to cool (and heat) homes in combination with an absorption heat pump (a brine/water heat pump) and PVT panels. During summer you warm up a closed water source and in winter you use that stored warmth in combination with the PVT panels and heat pump to warm your house. Towards the end of winter you stop using the PVT panels and warm your house by running the heat pump only of the source. This to create the ice you can then use in summer to cool your house. It's similar to a closed water source system with pipes in the ground with the difference that those don't get to temperatures low enough to create ice. Basically an ice system is a water source system that has been dimensioned too small. The advantage is that you can make use of the energy it takes to turn ice into water (and vice versa), something you can't with a water source. Melting (or creating) one kg of ice takes about as much energy as it takes to continue to heat that same kg of water to 80C afterwards, so there is a lot of energy being transferred in that phase change.
That phase change concept is something you also see in some passive temperature control products where during the day a material (a PCM or Phase Change Material that's made out of a mixture of salts) changes from solid to liquid by taking energy (heat) from a space (thus cooling that space) and at night, when temperatures drop below its melting point, it cools back down (gives off heat) into its solid shape ready to repeat the cycle. Basically they are thermal batteries, just like a closed water source is.

replies.author: @jeffreyrood8755
replies.text: @easy_s3351 great info. A thought along those lines would be to build a big enough system to have extra energy to heat and cool water. Much like the process of using this instead of a brake on a turbine. They instead use the extra energy to heat water or you could also freeze ice. I just got a styrofoam cooler from my wife's doctors office that would be otherwise thrown away. I will put 2 vent ducts in the the top with a fan blowing in to the cooler with ice for our bedroom at night. We run the fan now. And it will be used for this hopefully starting Saturday night.

author: @easy_s3351
text:
I always watch your videos with a lot of interest and I especially like the reason, the philosophy, behind why you do what you do. But I feel in this case I have to correct you. Your bellows machine is not a cheap diy Air Conditioning but a cheap diy ventilation unit or air handling unit (AHU). The difference being that an AC conditions the air in a certain space whereas an AHU  moves air from one space to another but doesn't actually condition the air. It may sound like a minor difference but it does explain why ACs are more expensive, there's a lot more to them. Systems to move air around have been around for a very long time but it wasn't till Mr. Carrier (they still make AC units) came up with the idea of running air through a curtain of cold water that the AC was born. I happen to be a HVAC engineer so I care about this kind of difference.
You talked about how in summer the tin roof of your building gets really hot, how about if you could use that heat? What I'm thinking about is using it to either run an absorption AC or an ORC/Stirling engine (or both). An absorption AC is much cheaper to run than a compressor driven AC because it doesn't have a compressor but a pump (as it pumps liquid around) which uses much less energy and it runs of excess/waste heat (usually industrial). The challenge would be to see if you could make one that'll work with the heat generated from your roof (the required size of the heat exchangers could be an issue). It could also be interesting to see if it could also work with PVT-panels, those solar panels that also generate heat (Photo Voltaic Thermal panels). 
That would be awesome as you'd then have a relatively cheap to run AC as opposed to the compressor driven ACs currently on the market. I mean, you want to cool when it's hot and the hotter it gets the better you can cool with an absorption AC whereas a compressor driven AC only has to work harder (and consume more energy) as it gets warmer. And you'd yield extra electricity from the PVT-panels because you're effectively cooling them which improves their performance, above 25C a PV panel generally looses 0.5% of its yield per degree C, and you're protecting them from overheating and subsequentially catching on fire. Possibly that extra electricity is enough to run the absorption AC of and then it'll become free to run. How's that for a sales pitch?
You could also use your roof's heat to run an ORC (Organic Rankine Cycle) engine or a Stirling engine when you don't want to cool or have enough cooling capacity with some extra heat left over.
I have actually been thinking about this for quite some time now but don't have sufficient knowledge to work it all out. I think you're a pretty smart and especially pragmatic guy and as you always point out we should share our ideas because several minds working together can achieve a lot more. And, as you also have pointed out, solutions will consist of several systems working together. So what I'm thinking is a system like I just described; PVT-panels on a roof (maybe we could use Oasis foam as a heat exchanger and make our own PVT-panels) and you use that heat to run an absorption heat pump (an AC is a heat pump). An extra benefit is that in the heat pump cycle after evaporating (with the heat from the PVT panels) you have to condensate at high temperatures and you could do that using cold tap-water, effectively (pre-)heating that water and creating hot tap-water. If, like I mentioned, you have enough cooling capacity you can then use any excess heat from the panels to run a Stirling/ORC engine (same principle). You could probably make such an engine with the air engine/Tesla Earthquake machine, put magnets in the piston and run a coil along the cylinder. You could also use the magnet in the piston to trigger a magnetic switch to operate the valve timing, then you could make it a closed system reducing the loss in efficiency due to leakage.
I think NH3 (ammonia) could be a good refrigerant to use in both the absorption heat pump (a water-ammonia solution) as well as in the Stirling/ORC engine. A water-ammonia solution is already the common thing to use in industrial absorption heat pumps. And ammonia has a nice expansion when evaporating as well as a low evaporation temperature (-33C at 1 Bar) so it could be useful in a Stirling/OCR engine. You could run the cycle at say 50C evaporation temperature (I think that's about 40Bar) and condensate at room temperature (25C at about 10Bar) But if using ammonia I think the Tesla machine would have to be adapted to run on it, it doesn't play well with certain other materials.
Well, quite an essay that's become. Let me know what you think.

replies.author: @GRBtutorials
replies.text: Indeed, I was thinking the same thing. The absorption refrigerator idea has actually already been implemented by Tech Ingredients on YouTube, so definitely feasible. You could even make a compression heat pump yourself, there are people on YouTube that have done exactly that, using second-hand compressors and propane as a refrigerant.

replies.author: @easy_s3351
replies.text:
​@GRBtutorials Thanks, I'll make sure to check out Tech Ingredients like you mentioned, sounds interesting. I know there's several companies who make absorption heat pumps for the domestic market, some of which in combination with PVT-panels (Vriessman, NIBE), but they're all quite expensive (understatement).
Pretty awesome that people are making their own compressor heat pumps, I'd never thought anyone would be doing that,  I'll also check out them out on YouTube. It'll be interesting to see how they went about it, especially what kind of parts they used and how they control the system. Cheers.

author: @NorthernKitty
text: I'm so glad the lonely pushrod finally found a home!!  I have a soft spot for orphans. 😊  I really like both the efficiency and the noise level of this.  I'm thinking you could build a nice little heater out of it.  What if you were to fit this to a chamber that preheated the air being drawn in?  This moves the air slowly enough that I think you could get some nice heat exchange happening.  The same may be true for making it into an evaporative cooler, sucking warm air through evaporative pads saturated with cold water and into a chamber filled with icy water before being blow out into the living space by the bellows.  Not to mention this would beat noisy fans in an RV to circulate air and reduce the risk of carbon monoxide buildup in the cabin.  (Edit)  Never mind me, I didn't watch your other video first where you discuss using the heat exchanger, LOL. 😄 I should know by now to wait until watching your next video before I shout out ideas to make sure you don't have a better one in mind first. 😋

replies.author: @ghostwheelppk
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering  To my mind, (and it depends a good deal on your local climate) Air conditioning also involves controlling humidity. In a desert area, there is no humidity so reducing temperatures with a swamp cooler will do. In the mid-Atlantic and South eastern coastal areas of the United States (for example) it's not just enough to reduce the temperature, but one of the primary functions of air conditioning is removing moisture from the air. Using (compressor) based climate control removes moisture and since there is heat associated with the free water vapor being removed, it is also reducing the temperature.

Wasn't it a side effect of the linear mechanical oscillator (configuration) that it was pumping air and the exhaust was lower in temperature as well?

Cheers!

Rocky Lee
